title: A Proof of the Fermionic Theta Coinvariant Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper proves the purely fermionic specialization of a Theta-operator conjecture for a supersymmetric diagonal coinvariant quotient with two sets of anticommuting variables. Its main theorem identifies the bidegree (i,j) Frobenius characteristic of the fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring FDR_n with Θ_i Θ_j ∇e_{n-i-j} evaluated at q=t=0, and proves that these components vanish for i+j≥n. The proof compares two recursive descriptions: a representation-theoretic hook-Kronecker formula for FDR_n due to Kim and Rhoades, and a Theta-operator recursion under the skewing operators h_j^⊥. The most useful content for hook-coefficient problems is the explicit expression of fermionic bidegrees as differences of Kronecker products of hook Schur functions, together with the general formula for h_j^⊥ applied to Kronecker products and the repeated use of the hook identity h_b e_a = s_(b,1^a)+s_(b+1,1^(a-1)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n≥1. Let FDR_n be the quotient of the exterior algebra generated by anticommuting variables θ_1,...,θ_n, ξ_1,...,ξ_n by the ideal generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants, where S_n permutes all indices diagonally. Let (FDR_n)_{i,j} denote the component of θ-degree i and ξ-degree j, and let Frob denote the ordinary Frobenius characteristic of an S_n-module. Let Θ_d mean the Theta operator Θ_{e_d}=Π composed with multiplication by e_d[X/M] composed with Π^{-1}, where M=(1-q)(1-t) and Π is the Macdonald eigenoperator with eigenvalue product over cells other than (1,1) of 1-q^{a-1}t^{b-1}; let ∇ be the Macdonald nabla operator with eigenvalue product over all cells of q^{a-1}t^{b-1}. Then (FDR_n)_{i,j}=0 whenever i+j≥n. When i+j<n, Frob((FDR_n)_{i,j}) = (Θ_i Θ_j ∇e_{n-i-j}) evaluated at q=t=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vanishing and a hook-Kronecker expression for Frob((FDR_n)_{i,j}) are imported from Kim-Rhoades as Theorem 3.2. The main work is to prove the general symmetric-function identity Θ_m Θ_ℓ ∇e_k evaluated at q=t=0 equals s_(k+ℓ,1^m)*s_(k+m,1^ℓ) minus s_(k+ℓ+1,1^(m-1))*s_(k+m+1,1^(ℓ-1)), where * is the Kronecker product and invalid hook terms are omitted. The proof is by induction on k+ℓ+m. Lemma 3.7 computes h_j^⊥ of the Theta side as a sum of lower-degree Theta expressions; the induction hypothesis converts all these lower-degree terms into hook-Kronecker differences. After reindexing and canceling the resulting eight families of terms, the expression becomes h_{k+ℓ-j}e_m * h_{k+m-j}e_ℓ minus h_{k+ℓ}e_{m-j} * h_{k+m}e_{ℓ-j}. Lemma 3.4 identifies this with h_j^⊥ of the hook-Kronecker side. Since equality holds after applying h_j^⊥ for every j≥1, and both sides have zero constant term unless k+ℓ+m=0, Lemma 3.1 forces equality. Substituting m=i, ℓ=j, and k=n-i-j gives the stated formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2, Kim-Rhoades hook-Kronecker formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring FDR_n defined as the quotient of the exterior algebra on θ_1,...,θ_n, ξ_1,...,ξ_n by the ideal generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants, the bidegree component (FDR_n)_{i,j} vanishes whenever i+j≥n. If i+j<n, then Frob((FDR_n)_{i,j}) = s_(n-i,1^i) * s_(n-j,1^j) - s_(n-i+1,1^(i-1)) * s_(n-j+1,1^(j-1)), where s_λ is a Schur function, * is the Kronecker product of symmetric functions corresponding to tensor product of S_n-representations, and the second term is interpreted as 0 when i=0 or j=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is cited from Jongwon Kim and Rhoades rather than reproved in the paper. The paper records that their proof shows the invariant element θ_1ξ_1+...+θ_nξ_n in the exterior algebra satisfies a bigraded Lefschetz-type property. That Lefschetz structure gives the quotient by positive-degree invariants and yields the difference of two adjacent hook-Kronecker products.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (3.43), central hook-Kronecker identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For all nonnegative integers k, ℓ, and m, with the convention that Schur functions indexed by invalid hooks contribute 0, one has the symmetric-function identity (Θ_m Θ_ℓ ∇e_k) evaluated at q=t=0 = s_(k+ℓ,1^m) * s_(k+m,1^ℓ) - s_(k+ℓ+1,1^(m-1)) * s_(k+m+1,1^(ℓ-1)). Here Θ_d=Θ_{e_d} is the Theta operator, ∇ is nabla, e_k is the elementary symmetric function, s_λ is a Schur function, and * is the internal Kronecker product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity is proved inside the proof of Theorem 1.2 by induction on N=k+ℓ+m. For N=0 both sides are 1. For N>0 both sides have zero constant term, so by Lemma 3.1 it is enough to compare h_j^⊥ of both sides for all j≥1. Lemma 3.7 gives h_j^⊥ of the Theta side as three sums of lower-degree Theta terms. Applying the induction hypothesis to these lower-degree terms produces sums of hook-Kronecker differences. The paper then groups the resulting terms into eight sums, reindexes four of them, and cancels all interior terms. The surviving boundary terms combine via h_a e_b = s_(a,1^b)+s_(a+1,1^(b-1)) into h_{k+ℓ-j}e_m * h_{k+m-j}e_ℓ - h_{k+ℓ}e_{m-j} * h_{k+m}e_{ℓ-j}. Lemma 3.4 proves that this is exactly h_j^⊥ of the hook-Kronecker right-hand side, completing the induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F and G be symmetric functions with vanishing constant terms. If h_j^⊥F = h_j^⊥G for every j≥1, where h_j^⊥ is the Hall-inner-product adjoint to multiplication by the complete homogeneous symmetric function h_j, then F=G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any partition λ=(λ_1,λ_2,...) with λ_1>0, write λ-bar=(λ_2,λ_3,...). Using adjointness, ⟨F,h_λ⟩ = ⟨h_{λ_1}^⊥F,h_{λ-bar}⟩. By hypothesis this equals ⟨h_{λ_1}^⊥G,h_{λ-bar}⟩ = ⟨G,h_λ⟩. Since F and G have zero constant terms, this covers every h_λ coefficient of positive degree; the h_λ form a basis of the ring of symmetric functions, so F=G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (3.3), h_j^⊥ as restriction and invariants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a finite-dimensional S_n-module and let 1≤j≤n. Regard Res^{S_n}_{S_j×S_{n-j}} V as a module for S_j×S_{n-j}. Then h_j^⊥Frob(V) = Frob(Hom_{S_j}(1_{S_j}, Res^{S_n}_{S_j×S_{n-j}} V)), where 1_{S_j} is the trivial S_j-module and the Hom space is an S_{n-j}-module through the second factor S_{n-j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Multiplication by h_j on Frobenius characteristics corresponds to inducing with the trivial S_j-module from S_j×S_{n-j} up to S_n. Since h_j^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by h_j under the Hall inner product, Frobenius reciprocity converts the coefficient pairing against an S_{n-j}-module into an S_j-invariant Hom space after restricting V to S_j×S_{n-j}. This yields the displayed Frobenius characteristic identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 1≤j≤n and let λ^(1), λ^(2) be partitions of n. Then h_j^⊥(s_{λ^(1)} * s_{λ^(2)}) = sum over μ⊢j and ν^(1),ν^(2)⊢n-j of c^{λ^(1)}_{μ,ν^(1)} c^{λ^(2)}_{μ,ν^(2)} (s_{ν^(1)} * s_{ν^(2)}). Here c^λ_{μ,ν} is a Littlewood-Richardson coefficient, s_λ is a Schur function, and * is the Kronecker product of degree n symmetric functions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the restriction-Hom interpretation of h_j^⊥ to V=S^{λ^(1)}⊗S^{λ^(2)}, where S^λ is the irreducible S_n-module of shape λ. Restriction commutes with tensor product, so Res(V) is Res(S^{λ^(1)})⊗Res(S^{λ^(2)}). By the Littlewood-Richardson branching rule, Res^{S_n}_{S_j×S_{n-j}} S^λ is the direct sum over μ⊢j and ν⊢n-j of c^λ_{μ,ν}(S^μ ⊠ S^ν). Tensoring two such decompositions gives summands (S^{μ^(1)}⊗S^{μ^(2)}) ⊠ (S^{ν^(1)}⊗S^{ν^(2)}). Taking Hom_{S_j}(1_{S_j}, -) contributes dimension 1 exactly when μ^(1)=μ^(2), by character orthogonality and self-duality of symmetric-group irreducibles, and contributes 0 otherwise. The remaining S_{n-j}-module has Frobenius characteristic s_{ν^(1)}*s_{ν^(2)}, giving the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (3.14), companion e_j^⊥ Kronecker recursion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the same notation as Lemma 3.3, e_j^⊥(s_{λ^(1)} * s_{λ^(2)}) = sum over μ⊢j and ν^(1),ν^(2)⊢n-j of c^{λ^(1)}_{μ′,ν^(1)} c^{λ^(2)}_{μ,ν^(2)} (s_{ν^(1)} * s_{ν^(2)}), where μ′ is the conjugate partition of μ and e_j^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by the elementary symmetric function e_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same as Lemma 3.3, except that e_j^⊥ corresponds to taking Hom_{S_j}(sign_{S_j}, -) rather than Hom_{S_j}(1_{S_j}, -). The sign representation appears in S^{μ^(1)}⊗S^{μ^(2)} with multiplicity 1 exactly when μ^(1)=(μ^(2))′, because tensoring an irreducible by sign conjugates its partition shape; otherwise the multiplicity is 0. Substituting this condition into the Littlewood-Richardson restriction sum gives the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (3.16), two-hook expansion of e_a h_b
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For nonnegative integers a and b, the product e_a h_b of an elementary and a complete homogeneous symmetric function expands as e_a h_b = s_(b,1^a) + s_(b+1,1^(a-1)), with the second term omitted when a=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the Pieri rule applied to s_(b) times e_a, equivalently adding a vertical strip of size a to a row of length b. The only hook-shaped outcomes are the hook with first row length b and leg length a, and the hook with first row length b+1 and leg length a-1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any j≥1 and integers k, ℓ, and m with k+ℓ+m=n, one has h_j^⊥(s_(k+ℓ,1^m) * s_(k+m,1^ℓ) - s_(k+ℓ+1,1^(m-1)) * s_(k+m+1,1^(ℓ-1))) = h_{k+ℓ-j}e_m * h_{k+m-j}e_ℓ - h_{k+ℓ}e_{m-j} * h_{k+m}e_{ℓ-j}. Here * is the Kronecker product, h_r and e_r are complete and elementary symmetric functions, and boundary terms with negative indices or invalid hooks are interpreted as 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof applies Lemma 3.3 to h_j^⊥ of a Kronecker product of two hook Schur functions. For a hook target s_(k+ℓ,1^m), the Littlewood-Richardson coefficient c^{(k+ℓ,1^m)}_{μ,ν} can be nonzero in the relevant sum only when both μ and ν are hooks. Write μ=(j-r,1^r). The Littlewood-Richardson rule gives the hook part of s_(j-r,1^r)s_(c,1^{n-j-c}) as s_(j-r+c,1^{r+n-j-c}) plus s_(j-r+c-1,1^{r+n-j-c+1}); all remaining terms are indexed by non-hook partitions and cannot contribute to a hook target. Thus the allowed ν are precisely the two hooks obtained by removing the j-cell hook μ from the arm or leg of the target hook. Doing this simultaneously for the two hook factors gives a sum that can be rewritten using e_a h_b = s_(b,1^a)+s_(b+1,1^(a-1)). Applying the same expansion to the adjacent hook product s_(k+ℓ+1,1^(m-1))*s_(k+m+1,1^(ℓ-1)) shifts the summation index, so all interior terms telescope. The two boundary terms are exactly h_{k+ℓ-j}e_m * h_{k+m-j}e_ℓ and -h_{k+ℓ}e_{m-j} * h_{k+m}e_{ℓ-j}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5, D'Adderio-Romero h_j^⊥ identity for Theta operators
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Θ_d=Θ_{e_d} be the Theta operator, let ∇ be nabla, and let H̃_k(x;q,t) denote the modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by the one-row partition k as used in the paper. Let [n choose r]_q be the q-binomial coefficient, equal to 0 outside 0≤r≤n, and define E_{N,K}(x;q)=q^K sum_{u=0}^K q^binom(u,2)[K choose u]_q(-1)^u e_N[X(1-q^{-u})/(1-q)]. For integers j,m,ℓ,k, the paper cites the identity h_j^⊥ Θ_mΘ_ℓ H̃_k(x;q,t) = sum_{r=0}^j [k choose r]_q sum_{a=0}^k sum_{b=1}^{j-r+a} Θ_{m-j+r}Θ_{ℓ+k-j-a}∇E_{j-r+a,b} · ( q^binom(k-r-a,2)[b-1 choose a]_q[b+r-a-1 choose k-a-1]_q + q^binom(k-r-a+1,2)[b-1 choose a-1]_q[b+k-r-a choose k-a]_q ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This result is quoted from D'Adderio and Romero, Theorem 8.2, and is not reproved in the paper. The present paper uses it as the Theta-operator counterpart to Lemma 3.4: after substituting a by k-a and then taking q,t to 0, it becomes the finite recursion stated in Lemma 3.7.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any integers m, ℓ, and k, (Θ_mΘ_ℓ∇e_k) evaluated at t=0 equals (Θ_mΘ_ℓ H̃_k(x;q,t)) evaluated at t=0, where H̃_k is the modified Macdonald polynomial for the one-row partition k in the paper's notation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, the paper uses the standard fact H̃_k(x;q,t)=∇e_k evaluated at t=0: the left side is the graded Frobenius characteristic of the ordinary coinvariant algebra of S_k with one bosonic variable set, while the right side is the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of the two-variable diagonal coinvariant algebra with one variable set specialized away. The remaining point is that applying Theta operators commutes with taking t=0 in the needed sense. The paper proves the more general claim that for any symmetric function G and any elementary-indexed Theta operator Θ_{e_λ}, (Θ_{e_λ}G)|_{t=0} = (Θ_{e_λ}(G|_{t=0}))|_{t=0}. It checks this on the Macdonald basis. If H̃_μ(x;q,0)=sum_ν D_{μ,ν}(q,t)H̃_ν(x;q,t), then D_{μ,ν}(q,0)=δ_{μ,ν}. Applying Θ_{e_λ} and setting t=0 gives the desired equality provided no poles occur at t=0. The absence of poles follows from the definition Θ_{e_λ}=Π e_λ[X/M] Π^{-1}: at t=0 the product e_λ[X/(1-q)]H̃_μ(x;q,0) expands in the modified Hall-Littlewood basis with coefficients regular in q, and the eigenvalue ratio Π_ρ/Π_μ has no pole at t=0. Applying this commutation twice gives the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any integers j,m,ℓ,k, with out-of-range indices interpreted as zero, h_j^⊥(Θ_mΘ_ℓ∇e_k) evaluated at q=t=0 equals the sum of three families: sum_{r=0}^j (Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r}) evaluated at q=t=0, plus sum_{r=0}^{j-1} ((Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r}+Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r-1})∇e_{k-j+2r+1}) evaluated at q=t=0, plus sum_{r=0}^{j-2} (Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+2}) evaluated at q=t=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Lemma 3.6 to replace ∇e_k by H̃_k at t=0, then apply Theorem 3.5. The paper first changes variables a to k-a in the D'Adderio-Romero formula. To evaluate at q=0, inspect the q-powers and q-binomial factors in the parenthesized coefficient. Nonzero contributions can occur only when a-r is -1, 0, or 1; the q-binomial factors also force r≤k and a≤j. Once a-r is fixed in these three cases, the surviving coefficient is independent of b, and the identity sum_{b=1}^N E_{N,b}=e_N collapses the b-sum to ∇e_N. If a=r-1, only one summand in the parenthesis survives and, after reindexing, gives sum_{r=0}^{j-1} Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+1}. If a=r+1, the symmetric contribution gives sum_{r=0}^{j-1} Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r+1}. If a=r, both parenthesized summands survive except at the two endpoints r=0 and r=j; splitting the endpoint correction yields sum_{r=0}^j Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r} plus sum_{r=0}^{j-2} Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+2}. Combining the three cases gives the displayed recursion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DR_n be the usual two-bosonic diagonal coinvariant ring and let TDR_n be the quotient of C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] tensor the exterior algebra on θ_1,...,θ_n, ξ_1,...,ξ_n by the ideal generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants. There is a canonical surjection DR_n⊗FDR_n onto TDR_n. Therefore the bidegree support vanishing (FDR_n)_{i,j}=0 for i+j≥n implies the corresponding fermionic bidegree support vanishing for TDR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The surjection is induced by multiplication of the bosonic and fermionic variables followed by quotienting by the larger invariant ideal defining TDR_n. In fermionic bidegree (i,j), the source is DR_n⊗(FDR_n)_{i,j}; if Theorem 1.2 gives (FDR_n)_{i,j}=0, then the source component and hence its quotient TDR_n component are also zero.

proof_techniques.name: Detect equality by complete skewing operators
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly proves equality of positive-degree symmetric functions F and G by showing h_j^⊥F=h_j^⊥G for all j≥1. Lemma 3.1 justifies this because the complete homogeneous functions h_λ form a basis, and the Hall-inner-product adjunction reduces every coefficient ⟨F,h_λ⟩ to a coefficient of h_{λ_1}^⊥F.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook Schur coefficient identities, one can try to prove a recursion under h_j^⊥ rather than compute coefficients directly. This is especially useful when both a Hilbert-series expression and a proposed combinatorial model have natural restrictions or deletion operations.

proof_techniques.name: Translate h_j^⊥ into restriction and S_j-invariants
proof_techniques.description: The identity h_j^⊥Frob(V)=Frob(Hom_{S_j}(1,Res^{S_n}_{S_j×S_{n-j}}V)) converts a symmetric-function operation into a representation-theoretic operation. In the paper, applying this to V=S^λ⊗S^μ gives a clean recursion for h_j^⊥ of a Kronecker product in terms of Littlewood-Richardson coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook coefficients c_λ(n) can be realized as Frobenius or Hilbert multiplicities of S_n-modules, this technique gives a way to derive recursive formulas by restricting from S_n to S_j×S_{n-j}.

proof_techniques.name: Kronecker-product recursion via Littlewood-Richardson branching
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 expresses h_j^⊥(s_λ*s_μ) as a sum over common S_j-shapes α of c^λ_{α,ν}c^μ_{α,ω}(s_ν*s_ω). The proof uses Littlewood-Richardson branching for each factor and character orthogonality to force the two S_j-shapes to agree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients often lead to hook Specht modules or exterior powers. This recursion reduces Kronecker products involving larger hooks to smaller Kronecker products, which can support inductive combinatorial interpretations.

proof_techniques.name: Hook telescoping through h_b e_a
proof_techniques.description: The identity h_b e_a=s_(b,1^a)+s_(b+1,1^(a-1)) lets adjacent hook Schur functions be packaged into a single product h_b e_a. Lemma 3.4 uses this packaging to make long sums of hook-Kronecker terms telescope, leaving only two boundary terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For coefficients indexed by hook partitions, grouping adjacent hooks into h_b e_a may simplify recurrences or reveal cancellation. This is a natural bridge between Schur-hook coefficients and exterior-variable constructions.

proof_techniques.name: Use fermionic variables to isolate hook-shaped representation data
proof_techniques.description: The fermionic quotient FDR_n is bigraded by two exterior degrees, and Kim-Rhoades' formula identifies each bidegree Frobenius characteristic as a difference of Kronecker products of hook Schur functions. The exterior variables encode hook shapes representation-theoretically, and the quotient by invariants produces the adjacent-hook subtraction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks specifically for hook-shape Schur coefficients in a multigraded Hilbert series. A promising transfer is to introduce anticommuting variables whose exterior degrees pick out hooks, then interpret the desired hook coefficient as the Hilbert series or dimension of an appropriate fermionic or bosonic-fermionic quotient.

proof_techniques.name: Specialize Theta-operator recursions at q=t=0
proof_techniques.description: The paper imports a general D'Adderio-Romero formula for h_j^⊥Θ_mΘ_ℓH̃_k and obtains a much simpler recursion after taking q,t to 0. The simplification comes from a q-adic survival analysis: only terms with a-r in {-1,0,1} remain, and the E_{N,b} sum collapses to e_N.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the universal coefficients c_λ(n) are connected to Theta or Delta operator formulas, specializing or extracting low-parameter limits may turn complicated symmetric-function expressions into tractable hook recurrences.

proof_techniques.name: Prove equality by matching two independent recursive structures
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1.2 does not compute either side directly. Instead, it obtains a recursion for the representation-theoretic hook-Kronecker side using Lemma 3.4, obtains a recursion for the Theta side using Lemma 3.7, and then shows the recursions agree with the same base case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients, it may be enough to show that the proposed objects satisfy the same h_j^⊥ or deletion recursion as the known Hilbert-series coefficients, avoiding a direct closed-form Schur expansion.

other_useful_info: Conjecture 1.1 of D'Adderio-Iraci-Vanden Wyngaerd, stated in the paper but not proved there, predicts for the full supersymmetric quotient TDR_n with two bosonic and two fermionic variable sets that the θ-degree i and ξ-degree j piece vanishes when i+j≥n and has bigraded Frobenius Θ_iΘ_j∇e_{n-i-j} when i+j<n; Theorem 1.2 proves the purely fermionic q=t=0 specialization. The paper points to Kim-Rhoades [12] for the Lefschetz proof and hook-Kronecker formula for FDR_n, to Rosas [15] for explicit Schur expansions of Kronecker products indexed by hook shapes, and to Jesse Kim-Rhoades [11] for a set-partition/noncrossing-set-partition model related to FDR_n. The introduction also notes that setting subsets of variables to zero in Conjecture 1.1 recovers the one-bosonic one-fermionic superspace coinvariant conjecture and a two-bosonic one-fermionic conjecture of Zabrocki, indicating that fermionic-variable methods are intended as a route from ordinary Schur coefficients to hook-controlled refinements.
why_relevant: Proves a fermionic case of the Theta-operator coinvariant conjectures, giving techniques for handling anticommuting variables in diagonal coinvariant quotients. These methods are relevant to proofs that pass from ordinary Schur coefficients to hook coefficients through fermionic variables.
